texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: ITERATIVE SOLUTION  OF MULTICOMPONENT PERMEATOR MODEL  EQUATIONS
texts.text: ITERATIVE SOLUTION  OF MULTICOMPONENT PERMEATOR MODEL  EQUATIONS

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: F.P.  McCANDLESS
texts.text: F.P.  McCANDLESS

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Department of  Chemical Engineering, Montana State University, Bozeman, MT  59717 (U.S.A.) (Received October 26,1987; accepted in revised form May 17,1989)
texts.text: Department of  Chemical Engineering, Montana State University, Bozeman, MT  59717 (U.S.A.) (Received October 26,1987; accepted in revised form May 17,1989)

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Summary
texts.text: Summary

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Countercurrent  and  recycle permeator  model equations  require a  trial  and  error  (iterative) solution procedure. Although the iterative  calculations are simple for a binary system, the calculations for a multicomponent  system are greatly complicated because there are M1 independent mole fractions  in an M component  system. This paper describes a systematic  method to find the multicomponent  solution vector using a direct search maximization  routine which is a modification of the complex method of Box.
texts.text: Countercurrent  and  recycle permeator  model equations  require a  trial  and  error  (iterative) solution procedure. Although the iterative  calculations are simple for a binary system, the calculations for a multicomponent  system are greatly complicated because there are M1 independent mole fractions  in an M component  system. This paper describes a systematic  method to find the multicomponent  solution vector using a direct search maximization  routine which is a modification of the complex method of Box.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Introduction
texts.text: Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The  flow pattern and use of recycle in a permeation  stage can have a significant  effect  on  the  separation  that  is obtained  in  the  stage.  As  a  result,  an analytical  understanding  of  these  effects  is  important  and  there  have  been several excellent  papers published  on the mathematical  modeling  of permeation  stages for the  separation  of binary  gas mixtures using different  flow  and recycle  configurations.  Among  these are papers by  Stern and co-workers [l31, Blaisdell and Kammermeyer [ 41, and Hwang et al. [ 5-81.
texts.text: The  flow pattern and use of recycle in a permeation  stage can have a significant  effect  on  the  separation  that  is obtained  in  the  stage.  As  a  result,  an analytical  understanding  of  these  effects  is  important  and  there  have  been several excellent  papers published  on the mathematical  modeling  of permeation  stages for the  separation  of binary  gas mixtures using different  flow  and recycle  configurations.  Among  these are papers by  Stern and co-workers [l31, Blaisdell and Kammermeyer [ 41, and Hwang et al. [ 5-81.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The  solution  of  the  countercurrent  model  equations  and  all of  the  recycle permeator equations requires a trial and error (iterative)  procedure. Typically, a reject composition  is assumed and the appropriate  differential  equations  are solved  numerically.  Iterations  are made  until  satisfactory  material balances are achieved  at the feed to the stage. For the case of a binary  feed, a Wegstein or similar routine  can conveniently  be used to provide  new trial values based on the results of previous  iterations and a solution is usually obtained  in a few cycles.
texts.text: The  solution  of  the  countercurrent  model  equations  and  all of  the  recycle permeator equations requires a trial and error (iterative)  procedure. Typically, a reject composition  is assumed and the appropriate  differential  equations  are solved  numerically.  Iterations  are made  until  satisfactory  material balances are achieved  at the feed to the stage. For the case of a binary  feed, a Wegstein or similar routine  can conveniently  be used to provide  new trial values based on the results of previous  iterations and a solution is usually obtained  in a few cycles.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: More  recently  the separation  of  multicomponent mixtures  (i.e., those  having three or more components) has become  of interest [6,9-U]. However,  for this case there is apparently no generally applicable, simple method to provide better  and better  (reject)  compositions  in the iterative calculations  which ul-

However,  for this case there is apparently no generally applicable, simple method to provide better  and better  (reject)  compositions  in the iterative calculations  which ul-

texts.label: text
texts.orig: timately  result in convergence.  With  A4 components  present  there are M1 independent  mole  fractions.  Although  constraints  are placed  on  the possible values of the individual mole fractions by material balance considerations, these are not too  helpful  in determining  the exact  composition  which  will result in convergence.  The  constraints  can only be used to somewhat narrow the range of possible values. As a result, the solution  of the multicomponent  model equations  is much more difficult  than for the binary  case.
texts.text: timately  result in convergence.  With  A4 components  present  there are M1 independent  mole  fractions.  Although  constraints  are placed  on  the possible values of the individual mole fractions by material balance considerations, these are not too  helpful  in determining  the exact  composition  which  will result in convergence.  The  constraints  can only be used to somewhat narrow the range of possible values. As a result, the solution  of the multicomponent  model equations  is much more difficult  than for the binary  case.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The  purpose  of the present paper is to describe a method  that we have successfully  used to solve the countercurrent  and recycle  multicomponent  model equations.  The  method  utilizes a direct search maximization  routine which  is a modification  of the complex  method  of Box.  The  equations and search technique will be described  only  for a simple countercurrent  stage but the method can easily be applied to other flow patterns and to recycle permeators.
texts.text: The  purpose  of the present paper is to describe a method  that we have successfully  used to solve the countercurrent  and recycle  multicomponent  model equations.  The  method  utilizes a direct search maximization  routine which  is a modification  of the complex  method  of Box.  The  equations and search technique will be described  only  for a simple countercurrent  stage but the method can easily be applied to other flow patterns and to recycle permeators.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As previously  discussed,  it is not  a trivial  matter  to  find  a set of  XOi  that results in the  solution  of  the  multicomponent equations,  but  we have  found that a modified version of the complex  method of Box  can easily be used in the calculations  to  obtain  convergence in  a  reasonable  number  of  iterations.  A brief description  of the procedure  will first be given followed by the calculation details.
texts.text: As previously  discussed,  it is not  a trivial  matter  to  find  a set of  XOi  that results in the  solution  of  the  multicomponent equations,  but  we have  found that a modified version of the complex  method of Box  can easily be used in the calculations  to  obtain  convergence in  a  reasonable  number  of  iterations.  A brief description  of the procedure  will first be given followed by the calculation details.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Search  method
texts.text: Search  method

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A number  of trial XOi  composition  vectors  ('vertices') are generated and the model equations  are solved for each of these compositions,  and the objective  function (material  balance) is evaluated  for  each  set. The  values  of  the objective  functions  are compared  and the XOi vector  giving the 'worst'  value is rejected. A new XOi vector  is calculated using the remaining 'better'  points in a specified manner. The cycle is repeated, rejecting the XOi vector that gives the worst value of the objective  function  and progressively calculating a better one until convergence  is obtained.
texts.text: A number  of trial XOi  composition  vectors  ('vertices') are generated and the model equations  are solved for each of these compositions,  and the objective  function (material  balance) is evaluated  for  each  set. The  values  of  the objective  functions  are compared  and the XOi vector  giving the 'worst'  value is rejected. A new XOi vector  is calculated using the remaining 'better'  points in a specified manner. The cycle is repeated, rejecting the XOi vector that gives the worst value of the objective  function  and progressively calculating a better one until convergence  is obtained.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The  reader is referred to the original paper by Box [ 12  ]  and to discussions of  this  method  in  textbooks [ 13,141 for  details  of  the  complex  method.  The features of a modified  version used in this study are presented below.
texts.text: The  reader is referred to the original paper by Box [ 12  ]  and to discussions of  this  method  in  textbooks [ 13,141 for  details  of  the  complex  method.  The features of a modified  version used in this study are presented below.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The  search  for  a solution (the  XOi  vector  that  results  in  convergence) is conducted  as follows:
texts.text: The  search  for  a solution (the  XOi  vector  that  results  in  convergence) is conducted  as follows:

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: I. Initial complex generation
texts.text: I. Initial complex generation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The search method requires that the number of vertices  (number  of XOi sets used in the search)  be no less than P=M+ 1, but it can be larger. For example, for a 4-component feed the number  of XOi vectors used in the search must be > 5.
texts.text: The search method requires that the number of vertices  (number  of XOi sets used in the search)  be no less than P=M+ 1, but it can be larger. For example, for a 4-component feed the number  of XOi vectors used in the search must be > 5.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: (a)  An  initial  XOi  vector,  XOi  (l), is assumed together  with estimates of possible  high and low values for  each compound.  These  values are 'educated guesses'.
texts.text: (a)  An  initial  XOi  vector,  XOi  (l), is assumed together  with estimates of possible  high and low values for  each compound.  These  values are 'educated guesses'.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: (b ) The  other (P 1) XOi vectors are generated randomly and sequentially using the equation

texts.text: Here  XOi (L)  and XOi (H) are the  high  and  low values  assumed in  (a),  and ri(K) is a random  number between 0 and 1. These X0;  vectors are normalized SO that CXOi=  1.

texts.orig: II. First objective evaluations
texts.text: II. First objective evaluations

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The  model equations  are solved  sequentially  using the P different  XOi  sets and the objective  function  is evaluated for each set. The  magnitude of the objective  functions  are compared,  and the XOi vector  giving the 'worst'  value is rejected.  The  centroid  of  all  of  the  XOi  except  the  rejected  one,  XOi(R), is determined  for each i:

The  magnitude of the objective  functions  are compared,  and the XOi vector  giving the 'worst'  value is rejected.  The  centroid  of  all  of  the  XOi  except  the  rejected  one,  XOi(R), is determined  for each i:

texts.text: (b)  The  objective  function  is evaluated using the new XOi vector  and compared with the rejected value.

texts.text: (c)  If the  new XOi  set gives an objective  function  value that is worse than the rejected one, a new XOi vector to try is found by contracting  the point  half the distance to the centroid:

texts.text: This  contraction  is repeated until the new objective  function  is 'better'  than the rejected one.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: IV.  Continued  search
texts.text: IV.  Continued  search

texts.label: text
texts.orig: After  the  new XOi  set gives a value of  the  objective  function  that  is better than  the  rejected  one,  the  cycle  is repeated,  that  is, rejecting  the  XOi  vector that gives the current  'worst'  value of the objective  function,  calculate  a new set by  reflection  through  the  centroid  of  the  remaining  XOi  sets, contraction of the vector  (if necessary),  etc. until the value of the objective  function  is less than a specified  small value.
texts.text: After  the  new XOi  set gives a value of  the  objective  function  that  is better than  the  rejected  one,  the  cycle  is repeated,  that  is, rejecting  the  XOi  vector that gives the current  'worst'  value of the objective  function,  calculate  a new set by  reflection  through  the  centroid  of  the  remaining  XOi  sets, contraction of the vector  (if necessary),  etc. until the value of the objective  function  is less than a specified  small value.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For this study the objective  function  used was
texts.text: For this study the objective  function  used was

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: OBJ =  5  ABS [ YOi (talc  ) -YOi  (material balance) ] i=l (13)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Convergence  is assumed when OBJ was less than  some small specified value. Other objective functions could easily be defined that are equally valid, however.
texts.text: Convergence  is assumed when OBJ was less than  some small specified value. Other objective functions could easily be defined that are equally valid, however.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Discussion
texts.text: Discussion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To test the method,  the calculations  of  Stern and Leone [lo] were repeated for the case of 20 o  C and dP=  3.45 x  lo5 N/ m2.  For these calculations  5 vertices were used with /3= 1.15. The  modified  Box  method  gave essentially the  same results as reported in Figs. 10 and 11 of that paper. Computer time for solution varied widely with stage cut and the initial estimate for the XOi vector:  from 58 to  217  cycles  with 100 to  318  evaluations  of  the  objective  junction  were required before  the convergence  criterion was met  (OBJ =  -  1.0 X 10m5).
texts.text: To test the method,  the calculations  of  Stern and Leone [lo] were repeated for the case of 20 o  C and dP=  3.45 x  lo5 N/ m2.  For these calculations  5 vertices were used with /3= 1.15. The  modified  Box  method  gave essentially the  same results as reported in Figs. 10 and 11 of that paper. Computer time for solution varied widely with stage cut and the initial estimate for the XOi vector:  from 58 to  217  cycles  with 100 to  318  evaluations  of  the  objective  junction  were required before  the convergence  criterion was met  (OBJ =  -  1.0 X 10m5).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The  complex  method  is a heuristic direct search technique  designed to find
texts.text: The  complex  method  is a heuristic direct search technique  designed to find

texts.label: text
texts.orig: the  maximum  value of  a function (in  this case  a minimum  is desired and so the objective  function  is made negative).  There  is no guarantee of convergence for a given initial estimate. However,  the method is easy to implement because of the simple search logic and with 'good'  initial estimates for XOi (1) , XOi (~5) and  XOi(H), it usually  converges  to  the  solution  in  a reasonable  number  of iterations.
texts.text: the  maximum  value of  a function (in  this case  a minimum  is desired and so the objective  function  is made negative).  There  is no guarantee of convergence for a given initial estimate. However,  the method is easy to implement because of the simple search logic and with 'good'  initial estimates for XOi (1) , XOi (~5) and  XOi(H), it usually  converges  to  the  solution  in  a reasonable  number  of iterations.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: P,  the  number  of  vertices  must be  greater than  or equal to  M+  1, but  the choice  of both  P and /3,  the reflection  parameter, is more or less arbitrary. Box in his original  paper  recommended  P1: 2M and p=  1.3  [  121. However,  in this work P= M+ 1 and p=  1.15 worked  satisfactorily,  but  it is certainly  possible that other combinations  would result in a solution using less computer  time.
texts.text: P,  the  number  of  vertices  must be  greater than  or equal to  M+  1, but  the choice  of both  P and /3,  the reflection  parameter, is more or less arbitrary. Box in his original  paper  recommended  P1: 2M and p=  1.3  [  121. However,  in this work P= M+ 1 and p=  1.15 worked  satisfactorily,  but  it is certainly  possible that other combinations  would result in a solution using less computer  time.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Occasionally  a 'better'  XOi (N'  ) vector  is not found  during the contraction step (s)  even after many contractions.  When  this happens the new vertex 'collapses' to the centroid  of the remaining XOi and under these conditions  progress toward the solution  cannot  be made. In this case the search is terminated and restarted using the 'best'  current set of XOi before  termination  together with new assumed high and low values. The  new search usually results in convergence,  but  occasionally  the  process  must be  repeated.  This  is similar to  a modification  suggested by Guin [ 151 which allows the search to continue when it would ordinarily be terminated.
texts.text: Occasionally  a 'better'  XOi (N'  ) vector  is not found  during the contraction step (s)  even after many contractions.  When  this happens the new vertex 'collapses' to the centroid  of the remaining XOi and under these conditions  progress toward the solution  cannot  be made. In this case the search is terminated and restarted using the 'best'  current set of XOi before  termination  together with new assumed high and low values. The  new search usually results in convergence,  but  occasionally  the  process  must be  repeated.  This  is similar to  a modification  suggested by Guin [ 151 which allows the search to continue when it would ordinarily be terminated.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: It would  also be possible  to generate the P  initial trial XOi vectors  without resorting to the use of  random  numbers.  For example,  the XOi  for the 'key' compound  could be specified  at P values over a specified  range and the other XOi  values  estimated,  based  on  the  magnitude  of  the  ~ii. This  concept  was briefly  investigated but it did not appear to offer  any advantages over the random number method.
texts.text: It would  also be possible  to generate the P  initial trial XOi vectors  without resorting to the use of  random  numbers.  For example,  the XOi  for the 'key' compound  could be specified  at P values over a specified  range and the other XOi  values  estimated,  based  on  the  magnitude  of  the  ~ii. This  concept  was briefly  investigated but it did not appear to offer  any advantages over the random number method.
